scenarios.chosen: Status quo plus incremental adaptation with explicit systemic teacher-training and monitoring safeguards

rejected.framing: Status quo plus incremental adaptation without systemic teacher-training reform
rejected.reason: The dossier's own mechanism section documents that multigrade viability is 'undermined by rural teacher shortages: half of Hungarian primary teachers are 50+' and 'unqualified-teacher rates are 2-3x higher in disadvantaged rural schools.' Choosing a framing that omits systemic teacher training would either force an unsupported assumption (that current supply is adequate) or conceal the core implementability constraint. The chosen framing makes the training requirement explicit and measurable, converting a latent assumption into a transparent design requirement.

rejected.framing: Status quo plus incremental adaptation positioned as equity-protective
rejected.reason: S1's equity_impact section establishes that 'Status quo without active school-choice regulation is therefore not equity-neutral' and risks 'entrench[ing] Hungary's dominant rural equity problem: between-school segregation.' A framing that positioned S1 as equity-protective would contradict the dossier's own evidence and conceal the equity costs. The chosen framing acknowledges S1 does not resolve equity harms, preserving the dossier's contested evidence status rather than upgrading it.

rejected.framing: Multigrade classrooms as a sustainable long-term solution
rejected.reason: The mechanism section cites evidence [strong] that 'an informal, legally unconfirmed ~14-pupil single-grade threshold means many micro-region cohorts will not sustain even multigrade viability without cross-settlement consolidation.' This evidence directly contradicts framing multigrade as a long-term standalone solution. The chosen framing positions multigrade as part of incremental adaptation, not as a definitive end-state, preserving the evidence that extreme depopulation may ultimately make multigrade unviable regardless of teacher quality.

scenarios.chosen: Structural grade reorganization with binding transport and receiving-school-quality guarantees as preconditions

rejected.framing: Polish 1999 structural reform as a proven template with predictable outcomes
rejected.reason: The dossier's evidence_status explicitly marks S2 as 'contested — The Polish structural template is strongly documented as historically implemented and later reversed, but its net academic and equity benefit remains genuinely contested.' A framing that positioned Poland as a proven template would upgrade contested evidence to strong and conceal the reversal (2017-2019) and persistent uncertainty about net benefit. The chosen framing acknowledges Poland's template is documented but conditional on binding quality and transport safeguards that the original Polish reform lacked.

rejected.framing: Upper-grade centralization as a neutral structural change
rejected.reason: The equity_impact section documents that 'without a statutory transport guarantee and subsidized route funding (transport is currently an unsubsidized municipal task that has led to service cancellations), commute burden will fall hardest on families least able to absorb it, risking a widening rather than narrowing of the dropout gap.' Framing upper-grade centralization as neutral would conceal the documented equity burden. The chosen framing makes transport and receiving-school quality explicit preconditions, converting latent equity risks into testable implementation requirements.

rejected.framing: Grade reorganization with Poland's funding model
rejected.reason: S2's mechanism addresses Polish funding and cost structure but the dossier provides no evidence that Hungary's fiscal capacity or per-pupil budget can absorb transport costs at Poland's scale. The cost_categories note 'a single route (Délegyháza precedent) cost ~35 million HUF/year for ~50 students' without specifying available budget line or financing source. The chosen framing limits claims to structural reorganization only, leaving financing design to S3 and S5, avoiding unsupported assumptions about fiscal feasibility.

scenarios.chosen: Networked retention through inter-municipal and association-run models contingent on legislative authority and capacity-building targeted at lowest-capacity municipalities

rejected.framing: Polish association-run models as directly transferable to Hungary without legal barrier resolution
rejected.reason: The mechanism section cites [evidence: strong] that 'Hungary's post-2013 tankerületi központ centralization removed municipal maintainer authority over most rural primary schools, creating a governance barrier to inter-municipal or association-run network models not present in the Polish or French comparators at the time of their adoption.' A framing that treated Polish models as directly transferable would conceal this documented legal barrier. The chosen framing explicitly conditions adoption on legislative authority creation, preserving the barrier as a design constraint rather than hiding it in assumptions.

The equity_impact section establishes that 'administrative-capacity burdens may systematically exclude precisely the villages most in need' and notes the Małopolska evidence that '25% of NGO-run rural schools still fell below sustainability thresholds.' A framing that positioned networking as inherently equity-protective would conceal the selection and exclusion risks. The chosen framing makes capacity-building targeted at lowest-capacity municipalities an explicit requirement, converting an equity risk into a design safeguard with measurable conditions.

rejected.framing: Multi-service hub models as a cost-efficiency solution
rejected.reason: The mechanism section cites the multi-service-hub component as [evidence: weak] and notes 'this specific combined-function model lacks direct outcome evaluation in the dossier.' A framing that emphasized hub models as a primary cost solution would upgrade weak evidence. The chosen framing positions hubs as a potential secondary benefit while anchoring implementation in the stronger evidence for inter-municipal networks and Polish-precedent association models, avoiding premature elevation of weak evidence.

scenarios.chosen: Consolidation with mitigation only where destination-school quality and transport funding are independently verified adequate before closure

rejected.framing: Consolidation as a cost-saving measure with TEIP-style support as offsetting mechanism
rejected.reason: The evidence_status section marks S4's core mechanism as 'contested' and the mechanism cites that Portugal's TEIP 'reduced dropout and failure rates in targeted schools over its multi-cycle history, yet the same evaluation body found it failed over 15 years to close the overall performance gap.' A framing that positioned TEIP support as a full offset would upgrade contested evidence to moderate. The chosen framing positions TEIP support as a partial mitigation requiring independent verification of destination-school quality before closure, preserving the contested evidence status and the documented partial failure.

rejected.framing: Closure as a necessary structural solution despite equity costs
rejected.reason: The cost_categories and political_risks sections document that closure effects are only visible 'significantly after 5-6 years' per Danish/Finnish evidence, and that current Hungarian law treats closure as 'the maintainer's exclusive prerogative with only 'interest injury' (not legal injury) available to parents.' A framing that normalized closure necessity would conceal both the accountability mismatch and the lack of binding parental recourse. The chosen framing positions closure only as a conditional option, pending verification of mitigating conditions, avoiding normalization of an inequitable legal prerogative.

rejected.framing: Statutory transport and TEIP support as sufficient equity safeguards
rejected.reason: The equity_impact section establishes that even with these measures, closure 'still likely produces a net equity cost for the most disadvantaged families relative to well-resourced retention alternatives, unless destination-school quality and transport funding are both independently verified as adequate.' A framing that positioned transport and TEIP as sufficient safeguards would contradict the dossier's own evidence. The chosen framing makes independent verification a precondition, converting an assumption into a testable requirement.

scenarios.chosen: National systemic framework with binding statutory thresholds, independent oversight, and disaggregated registry as accountability mechanisms

rejected.framing: National framework as an advisory registry to support evidence-based local decisions
rejected.reason: The mechanism section cites [evidence: contested] that 'an advisory, non-binding national registry and threshold system, absent enforcement teeth, risks being used selectively to legitimize pre-determined closure decisions rather than genuinely informing them, since Hungarian law already treats closure as the maintainer's exclusive discretionary prerogative.' A framing that positioned the framework as advisory only would risk conceding the registry to selective instrumentalization. The chosen framing makes binding statutory force and independent oversight explicit, converting an assumption (neutral use) into an enforceable requirement.

rejected.framing: Framework as primarily a teacher-training standardization mechanism
rejected.reason: The mechanism section cites teacher-training standardization as [evidence: moderate] but notes 'the ~4-year training pipeline lag limits how quickly this mechanism can take effect.' A framing that emphasized training standardization as the framework's primary function would upgrade its relative contribution beyond the evidence and understate the evidentiary gap addressed by the registry/thresholds component (cited as [evidence: weak]). The chosen framing positions teacher training as a secondary component, preserving evidence proportionality.

rejected.framing: Framework as a transparency tool absent enforcement risk
rejected.reason: The equity_impact section establishes that 'an advisory, aggregate registry could equally be co-opted to legitimize inequitable closure decisions already favored by tankerületi központok, particularly against the poorest, least-organized villages.' A framing that positioned the framework as purely transparency-producing would conceal the risk of instrumentalization. The chosen framing makes independent audit of selective invocation and disaggregation by Roma/non-Roma and settlement-poverty tier explicit requirements, converting a transparency mechanism into an accountability mechanism.
